cdp.name: CDP
cdp.gloss: Customer Data Platform — the unified store of who your customers are.

perso.name: Personalization (1:1)
perso.gloss: Tailoring the message to the individual, not the segment.

genai.name: Generative AI
genai.gloss: Models that produce content — copy, images, replies — from a prompt.

agentic.name: Agentic AI
agentic.gloss: Systems that don't just generate, they decide and act toward a goal.

decis.name: Agentic decisioning
decis.gloss: An agent choosing what to say, when, how often and on which channel — per person.

bandit.name: Bandits / RL
bandit.gloss: The maths that lets a system learn which choice wins by trying and measuring.

cep.name: CEP
cep.gloss: Customer Engagement Platform — where this messaging actually runs.

geo.name: GEO
geo.gloss: Generative Engine Optimization — being visible inside AI answers.

govern.name: Governance
govern.gloss: The controls that make automated decisions safe, explainable and legible.

stories.head: Salesforce's Agentforce struggles reveal data and readiness barriers to marketing AI agents
stories.plain: Slow uptake of Salesforce's agentic AI product exposes a wider truth: most enterprises aren't ready because their data quality is too poor and operations too siloed. This matters for anyone evaluating agentic marketing tools — the technology isn't the bottleneck, the foundation underneath it is.

stories.head: Survey: 91% of marketers claim an AI search strategy, but most lack real execution
stories.plain: A 600-person survey finds a big gap between confidence and action on AI search visibility: 91% claim a strategy, but 62% admit they're already behind competitors. This signals that GEO is moving from optional to urgent, and most teams don't yet have the playbook to compete inside AI-generated answers.

stories.head: Marketers know post-click experience drives paid media ROI but chronically underinvest in it
stories.plain: New research shows marketers pile budget into targeting and AI-powered ad tools while neglecting landing pages and post-click journeys they themselves identify as the top ROI driver. The finding has direct implications for how martech budgets and personalization priorities should be set.

stories.head: Stack rationalization and modular content are quietly reshaping martech beyond the AI hype
stories.plain: While AI dominates headlines, the real operational shifts are platform consolidation, modern measurement, and modular content architecture. This piece gives buyers and builders a framework for what decisions actually matter when evaluating martech stacks in 2026.

stories.head: AI commoditizes content, making trust — not information — the new marketing edge
stories.plain: When generative AI can produce any informational content instantly, differentiation shifts to credibility and trust signals. The article argues marketers must rethink content strategy from the ground up — a foundational governance and brand question, not just a tactical one.

stories.head: Salesforce's Agentforce struggles reveal marketing's agentic AI readiness gap
stories.plain: Slow uptake of Salesforce Agentforce exposes a wider enterprise problem: poor data quality and operational unreadiness are the real blockers to agentic AI, not the technology itself. This matters because it reframes the buying conversation — companies that skip data foundations will waste budget on agents that cannot act reliably.

stories.head: 5 questions to ask AI vendors before you buy anything
stories.plain: A practical procurement checklist covering business-value proof, data policies, customer evidence, and implementation realities. Essential reading for anyone evaluating martech AI purchases, because it shifts the burden of proof back onto vendors and gives buyers concrete criteria to cut through hype.

stories.head: 91% of marketers claim an AI search strategy, but most lack real execution
stories.plain: A survey of 600+ marketing and PR professionals finds a sharp gap between stated confidence and actual practice in AI search visibility, with 62% saying they are already behind competitors. The data quantifies how urgently marketers need to move from claiming a GEO strategy to actually building one.

stories.head: Why AI-ready data infrastructure is so hard to build — and how to start
stories.plain: Many teams stall trying to fix all their data before deploying AI. This piece argues for starting with one high-value problem rather than boiling the ocean. Directly relevant to anyone budgeting for CDP or AI projects, because it sets realistic expectations and a practical sequencing approach.

stories.head: Agentic AI hype vs. reality: virtually no enterprise-wide deployments yet exist
stories.plain: An analysis of agentic AI adoption finds that all known deployments are limited to a single function or business unit — no enterprise-wide rollout has been publicly documented. This calibrates expectations for buyers and builders who are being sold on fully autonomous marketing operations that do not yet exist at scale.

month.skicker: 30-day synthesis · the shape underneath the feed

themes.head: Agentic AI's reality gap is now a boardroom problem
body: Autonomous AI agents are being sold as the next operating layer for marketing, but the month's coverage reveals a widening gap between the pitch and the payoff. Salesforce's publicised difficulties underscore that agentic systems fail in practice when the underlying data, workflows, and governance aren't already mature — and most organisations' aren't.

The pressure to move past hype toward measurable ROI is real. Analysts and practitioners alike are now asking vendors to prove value before contracts are signed, signalling that the 'adopt first, fix later' era is closing. Decisions made now about which agentic platforms to trust will lock in competitive positions for years.
ev: Agile Brand · ROI from agentic AI

themes.head: AI-ready data infrastructure as the real competitive moat
body: Ninety percent of organisations claim an AI strategy, but the month's reporting makes clear that data infrastructure — not the AI layer itself — is the actual constraint. Building pipelines that are clean, connected, and governed enough to feed autonomous systems remains genuinely hard, and most enterprises are still mid-journey.

This shifts the strategic conversation away from tool selection toward data architecture investment. The 'Frankenstack' of siloed point solutions is now a liability: platforms like Creatio are positioning integrated, low-code stacks as the antidote, and the market is listening. Whoever owns the data plumbing owns the AI advantage.

themes.head: Content is commoditised — distribution & context win now
body: AI generation tools have collapsed the marginal cost of producing text, images, and video to near zero, and the month's analysis names the consequence plainly: content quality alone is no longer a differentiator. The new battleground is where and how content reaches audiences — especially as AI search reshapes discovery and reduces direct web traffic.

Research shows 91% of marketers claim an AI search strategy, yet most have only surface-level plans. Paired with new one-image e-commerce workflows and AI-powered video agents, the implication is clear: teams must redirect effort from production volume toward contextual relevance, channel orchestration, and the owned-audience relationships AI cannot replicate.
ev: MarTech · content no longer an advantage
MarTech Series · AI search strategy gap

themes.head: SaaS economics fracture — services & outcomes replace licences
body: The traditional SaaS model — charge for seats, ship features, grow ARR — is under structural pressure. This month's analysis argues that software alone no longer justifies vendor margins as AI capabilities become table stakes across every platform. Vendors are pivoting toward bundled services, outcome-based pricing, and embedded intelligence to defend revenue.

For buyers, this is a moment of leverage: procurement conversations can now demand demonstrable business outcomes rather than feature checklists. Acquisitions like Brunner-AdSkate signal that agencies are also consolidating AI capability to compete with pure-software vendors, blurring the line between martech and marketing services.
ev: MarTech · look past AI to see martech's future

themes.head: The human-skills crisis hiding inside AI adoption
body: As AI handles more execution, a quiet crisis is forming: junior marketers are losing the repetitive work that used to build expertise, and organisations have no clear plan for replacing that learning path. The generational divide in AI attitudes — Gen Z simultaneously the most enthusiastic adopters and the loudest sceptics — adds urgency to the question of what human judgment is still for.

This isn't a soft HR issue; it's a strategic risk. If the next cohort of senior marketers never develops deep craft skills, the human oversight that makes agentic AI trustworthy disappears. Teams and CMOs need deliberate apprenticeship models and 'human edge' frameworks before the institutional knowledge gap becomes irreversible.
ev: Agile Brand · Gen Z embracing & alarmed by AI
Agile Brand · human edge in automated CX

month.takeaway: Across every story this month, the underlying shift is the same: AI is moving the competitive advantage from what you produce to the infrastructure, judgment, and human depth behind the production — and most organisations are only beginning to reckon with that.
